sentences.text: OK.
There was that one day that we worked at-- that I-- we had to work at the-- carrying bricks to and fro the railway station, and witnessing the arrival and selection of a Hungarian transport.
I mention too-- ironic-- that when the people arrived, the impression they had, whatever they had-- they came with garden chairs, with lawn chairs, with umbrellas.
And so they-- it was all down there on the-- on the railway tracks.
They thought they maybe-- whatever they thought.
It was a bit of disillusionment, actually, for them.
That was that only day that we had to work.
[INAUDIBLE] No.
There was also very-- moments for me of-- of desperation.
I was alone.
People were involved with themselves or whoever had groups.
I was a child and didn't belong-- how you say [NON-ENGLISH].
It was just moving around with actually not-- few people I could talk to, but not too good.
I mean, how could you describe that, the state you were in?
There was electrified wire around Auschwitz and I knew that.
And-- I didn't do it.
No.
But there was many occasion of temptation for me to go to the wire and to end it, one and for all.
As mentioned, actually, I didn't do it.
Then, there was-- a couple of weeks later, there was also an incident that there was-- I heard from somebody who was in the sick barrack that my-- my cousin and his sister were in the sick barrack.
And although we didn't have much contact with them in normal life, I wanted to visit them.
And the date that I marked that it was able to me to squeeze out somehow to go to visit them in the sick barrack was the date the German choose for us to-- to leave Auschwitz.
We didn't know at that time, but we were called in a big room.
And there was the usual customary, take off your clothing and stand upright for the SS so they can look you up and down.
I was so nervous.
I remember vividly I developed a rash all over my body.
And then I thought-- and that compounded it.
I said, oh my god, they see that rash, they think I have an infectious disease.
Now it's done with me completely.
Then I see one as he comes to me and he looks me up and down.
And-- because everybody had to be separately-- I don't know-- looked up and down.
He said, OK, go.
So I-- I was [INAUDIBLE] safe.
I-- I didn't have to stay.
We all left Auschwitz, or Birkenau, at that time then.
And we're put on a train.
And naturally, you're not going-- we are not-- you are not being told where you're going.
We arrived at a place and we had to work.
The place was called Reichenbach.
R-E-A?
R-E-- It was called Reichenbach in Germany.
And there, when we were lead to the barracks, we found that the people-- the rest of the Philips people were there already, who left us four days after they arrived in Auschwitz.
Naturally, I mean, we were very, very glad.
And we were one, more or less, family or group again.
We were put to work in Telefunken.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But-- and-- but the camp we were was quite a distance from Telefunken.
But we had to walk through the snow-- and in the winter, was it?
Yes.
We left Auschwitz in [INAUDIBLE] August.
Yes.
And we were in June-- six week, July, August, we left Auschwitz.
And we walked all the way to the factory.
The factory was work and a huge factory.
It was, more or less, in kind of a glass cage, more or less the same work as what I did in Holland.
But I couldn't work too well.
We had 12-hour shifts.
We had barely enough to eat, barely enough sleep.
Our sleep was interrupted by the bombings what the Air Force did, the English Air Force.
So they didn't produce.
And many time that I just remember falling asleep, and then one of those supervisors came, and oh, [INAUDIBLE], You're up again and you woke up again.

We-- there was there, naturally, a washroom, and it was the communication room where we get the latest news, what is the war, when it's finished, and how-- and where are the Russians and, you know, what's happening.
We were timed when we went past the desk-- son of a gun, one of the supervisors.
And so we were timed that we're not too long, because naturally, they knew what was going on.
There was also one woman there.
She was herself and-- not from the camp.
She lived there.
But she was also maybe forced to work there.
She was very, very nice.
She called me by-- I'd say Miss Mossel, you know, you have no name, nothing, so Fraulein or Miss Mossel.
She had respect and she felt for us, but there's not much she can do.
Some other women I know got something, but they were older.
They knew how to work the ropes.
I didn't.
So that was our time there.
I developed typhus there at the time from drinking the water in Auschwitz, because they told us while we were standing there that whole night in the shower room in Auschwitz, don't drink the water.
Well, we were thirsty.
We didn't get anything, so we drunk.
And later on, I developed typhus.
I was very, very sick.
With me in the sick barrack was another girl, a girl from my street.
She was dying.
And so the one who are dying, they were transported back to Auschwitz.
There was also one girl from Philips, who was a naughty girl, got pregnant by one of the capos in Feucht.
She was starting to show.
Back to Auschwitz, and cremated.
That was Reichenbach.
There was not-- beside the bombings and the workings and the deprivation of sleep or hunger, you existed there.
Then there was one night, [INAUDIBLE] August-- we remained in Reichenbach around 'til-- I don't know-- the middle or the end, but until February 1945.
No.
[LAUGHS] No.
Nothing.
I think so.
I think so.
I think so, with [INAUDIBLE] the young girls together-- younger girls.
I was the youngest, one of the teenagers, or 18-, 19-years-old together.
No.
No, no.
Not like that.
Maybe-- not that I-- not that I know of, put it that way.
I don't think there was any perk for advancement anyway, because what's the use of an advancement?
You're in the same boat, the same rotten boat, the Germans are with you.
Then the war started to come closer and closer.
We heard the fighting.
We heard shooting during the night, the bombing.
And we made jokes already.
The Russians are going to liberate us.
That was January, February 1945.
I now read now that January '45, Auschwitz was liberated.
Well, we didn't know.
We were a distance from there.
Then we did one-- one night our group with girls didn't do too much work.
We-- as usual, we didn't know that.
We didn't do too much work.
We went home during the night.
We went in our bed.
We were taken from our bed in the morning And?
Everybody had to stand on appell.
And they had to-- 13 girls, including me, were chosen to have punishment, because we didn't work.
We didn't make our production.
There was one women there and she was the spokeswoman for us-- put herself up for spokeswoman.
And we could choose to 25 beatings on your rear end or cutting off your hair.
Well, she managed to make that we-- our hair was cut off.
So we had-- [INAUDIBLE] was a camp close by-- [NON-ENGLISH], very well known, too, was in the neighborhood there, when one man to-- had to cut off all our hair.
We were allowed to have something on our head while we were walking from the barracks to the factory.
But while we where in the factory, we had to show the people what we were doing and how we were punished.
Well, OK.
That's what we did.
So all kinds of things we put on our head.
And that was one incident.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was incidents-- you know, was very, very primitive with washrooms.
There was no washroom.
There was just-- it was a kind of a-- a covered-- a covered barrack, you say, what you have sometimes too called latrines, what soldiers maybe have.
It's a hole with a pail underneath.
Everybody had his duty done and you had to carry the pail.
And sometimes you slipped and it was icy.
There was no way that you properly had soap or whatever.
I wondered sometimes that not more illnesses or sicknesses occurred in that time.
But then, we had maybe the luck that there was no-- was cold, cold, cold.
That's one incidents too.
There was incidents, you say it's-- it's comic-tragic.
A woman, she was together there with her sister-in-law.
And they-- you know, they slept together in the bed.
And one morning, the woman said to her sister, oh, she said, I had a warm bath.
It was in a wheelbarrow full of urine and she washed herself.
Now she screamed when she found out.
So it's tragi-comic.
So that was the life there, 'til they told us-- or told us-- headed us in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to leave.
And we went into walk for 100 kilometers into the-- how you say-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- the Owl Mountains, you call it-- through snow.
We first slept in where they keep the-- in horses' stables.
And one occasion, I know we just left the horse stables in the morning.
There was one consolation-- the SS slept with us there too.
They had no other accommodation, so they had to walk with us, just the same.
They got a little bit better, more food.
But they walked with us.
And after we left that horse stable, the-- it was bombed.
And then we walked on the-- on the mountains.
I have actually no recollection what clothes, what shoes, nothing what I had on that time.
That was another time that I felt desperate and wanted to sit down in the snow and said I had enough.
Some girls were together.
I met them later on.
They said, come on, come on.
So they pushed me up, and let's go.
From then on, we went to, successively, two camps.
And actually, not camps.
They were not actually concentration camps.
They were just barracks where they could put us up for the night.
Then we-- how we started to go by car-- by cattle car, I don't know.
After that walk, we went from one [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sleeping place-- put it that way-- to another.
We came on one, and it was called Bendorf.
It was a salt mine.
And there I met my-- met the mother of my girlfriend and were we to see, but I lost her again.
We left-- we left that camp, then after a couple of days again.
And they took-- the Germans took-- emptied the camp and took all kinds of clothes invested with lice onto us and we had to carry it.
There were also Gypsies left the camp, and they were with us on the transport.
Then that was a transport there-- from there to another place.
There was a woman there, the wife of a Dutch comedian.
The Gypsies were positioned by the Germans into the cattle car as to-- so they could do the dirty work.
They had to do the dirty work.
I don't know-- the Gypsies had displeasure with the woman, maybe didn't like her.
And she was in the wagon next to me.
They tortured her to death with putting drops of water or letting it subtly as the woman screams and screams.
And the scream of the woman was-- for days, was horrible.
Well, finally, naturally, they killed her.
I had to sit with the Gypsies too once, because-- I don't know what I did.
It was punishment, so they pushed and they pummeled me.
But that was it.
There was a dividing line.
There was the entrance and we were there, and The gypsies were on the other side.
So as punishment, I had to sit in between them.
So then I came back to the own one.
There was no water.
And if there was water, the SS or the women or men just threw the water into the cattle car, but didn't give us to drink.
So the women, in desperation, started to drink their own urine.
You had to have something.
And I still remember having it in my hand and then the train made a sudden movement.
It fell out of my hand, and I never drank it.
I also vividly remember that I had-- how do you say it?-- delusions, or I went out of it, because I know exactly feeling.
I was there, but I was walking in a-- like your conscious does that, was walking in a big, big room, just wandering around.
I still remember that feeling.
Although, because you know, we were so tightly packed together and that's why you escape.
And that feeling I remembered very much, walking in a big, big, big room.
Because, well-- So finally, we somewhere ended.
They stopped.
And they opened the doors and we could get out, and then had some water and we are outside.
Then, in another place-- continued our travel.
Another place, we were let out on a grassy area more or less, in a bush.
There we were able to wash a little bit and to wander around too, although, we had to be careful, for-- because there was still air raids and so on going on.
That was Bendorf.
We were also close by a town in East Germany, still from Leipzig.
And the train had to stop because of engine trouble.
And in the meantime, they bombarded the train station in Leipzig.
Later on, we were-- we could travel further and it was all February, March 1945.
We were also-- and then at that time, we were in open cattle wagons and it was freezing.
We were standing the night in front of the camp of Bergen-Belsen.
And I remember they wanted to get rid of us.
And they asked, like in a hotel, we want those people in there, those women.
And Bergen-Belsen they-- it was too full, packed, no accommodation.
So they had to carry us further.
Then we went to-- [INAUDIBLE] several names-- and then we went to last one was-- not the last one-- one of the last ones was Porta Westfalica.
That was-- as I remember, we had appell outside in a-- in a green grassy area.
It was nice.
You saw the nature.
I remember when-- once when we were on appell, there was people there on the mountain.
They were filming us.
And the Germans saw that, and they put the dogs and-- up to the people, you know, to see what was-- because we were a ragged bunch, naturally, at that time.
They tried to put us to work in the caves, because originally there was a Telefunken and there was equipment there.
I mean, the machines were there, but equipment was not.
It was I think the end of March, beginning of April.
Everything was--
So we sat there.
We sat there and didn't do anything.
It was not bad.
You come a little bit too rest.
How long we were there, no idea of time.
Then from Porta Westfalica we probably went to another one.
And then our end was we ended up in Hamburg in kind of a-- on the outside of the city of Hamburg.
I know the name of the street still too.
Some barracks was there.
There we were with four in a bunkbed.
And we had a little piece of bread to be divided into four, because they didn't have food and we didn't have any food.
So it was nothing.
We were near the end.
If they would have waited for six weeks and left us there, we would have all died.
That was in April, 1945.
And we were on the way, on the go, from February, two months, back and forth, and the conditions.
Then we heard a rumor that we were going to Sweden.
And I said, what?
I didn't even know anymore where Sweden was.
We were gone.
Yes, it was true.
And on-- the end of April, I think 20, 20-whatever, April, we were put on the train with some German-- old German soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't do a-- whatever-- the SS.
And they guided us to the border of Denmark and Germany.
And they were very sorry that we left.
We didn't.
They wanted to go with us.
Get out.
So there in Denmark, we were awaited-- I think with the queen or the princess, whoever was in-- in power at that time.
And the Red Cross was waiting for us.
It was the same amount of people, almost, as far as I know.
Maybe one--
Yes.
Yes.
As I said, began-- and I cannot emphasize, the Philips saved our lives.
And I compare now what I hear from We were the Phillips group.
It was our Schindler's List when we started to work in a factory in-- in Feucht.
There's no way, because personally, individually, I would have never made it, definitely not, as a child, a teen.
No way.
So then we arrived in Denmark.
And the Red Cross was so good and so nice.
But they were-- what did they know?
They gave us-- they gave us the purest cream, and well, it was disaster.
And I don't know how many died, but we were all very, very, very sick.
So then, the next day, they started to put us on ration.
The fact that we were-- came out with another transportive group too later on-- independent from us were some women of Ravensbruck.
They had the same deal as we.
We were exchanged with German prisoners of war who were still in Sweden.
And near the end of the war, they still thought they could win something if they have those old soldiers back.
So they said, OK, with a bunch of women, let-- get the soldiers back.
And Count Folke Bernadotte was instrumental with Himmler to make the deal.
I think they made some other deals too, but that was the deal that concerned us.
And we were exchanged and we came into Sweden.
But Folke Bernadotte later on, as I said, was killed in King David Hotel in Israel [LAUGHS] by the-- by [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Then we stayed not long there.
We wear transported from Copenhagen by train to the boat to go across Sweden.
We were ashamed-- I was ashamed to sit in that boat.
It was-- you know, it was like you're starting to be normal again, maybe, you know?
We were treated that way.
We had clothes that was full of lice.
At that time they managed finally to get it.
I was one thing lucky.
I had no hair, so no lice could come on my hair.
Should be grateful for that.
Whatever we could get rid of our clothing, we threw in the water when we were on our trip to Sweden.
We arrived in Sweden and were cleaned, and had showered and new clothing.
And everyday, we-- we got some clothing.
We were fed very slowly on a diet 'til our stomachs were-- were used to it.
Also in a kind of a camp in Malmo-- in Malmo, in Sweden.
And they treated us very, very, very, very well, very well treated.
Then we went to another camp near Goteborg in Sweden.
And there, most of the women, well, recovered.
Some women had typhus from the lice, [INAUDIBLE], scabies, well, all the kind of things.
I had also eczema all over the place from a-- but the funny part what there-- not funny part, but a part was that we had-- we had sheets from paper.
Sweden was known for paper.
So every time we clean ourselves with the sheets, we broke the sheets.
Then we went in a camp.
We started back to-- to a little bit normal life.
A group of girls, the ones who put me up from the snow, we got acquainted with a Swedish family, who more or less adopted us.
And we visited them and we went to-- to a fair and we came a little bit back-- back to life.
Then I-- I became ill.
I had pleurisy in the worst form.
My lungs were inundated with water.
It was on the border of TB, but I didn't have.
So they sent me to a part more in the-- in the middle of Sweden.
And there I was in a hospital with all the other sick people from the camp.
The whole world was there, Hungarian, Romanian, Polish.
I learned a smattering of-- of all those languages, and Swedish too.
We got food.
And then I was-- yeah, well, aspirated.
They aspirated the fluid of my lungs.
And as soon as that was done, I felt fine.
And I ate and ate.
They had five times we got fed.
I ate for 10 times, naturally.
But we ate and ate and ate.
It was-- that was-- it was a very good time.
And it was-- it was fine.
And so I stayed there 'til I think in August 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And most of women-- most of the women left already for Holland the three months we were in Sweden.
So we left-- they left for Holland.
And I was in a-- in that hospital.
Then I was released in the hospital-- from that hospital and went to another recovery camp.
And there also I met the whole world.
And I had quite a good time there.
Yes.
A-L-I-N-G-S-A-S with a little circle-- it was Swedish-- on the top.
Yes.
I have pictures from there too.
Had a very good time.
I met people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and came a little bit back to life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed there 'til January, 1946.
In the meantime, every month, I got a little allowance from the Dutch-- from the Dutch government, a little money.
So I had something.
Otherwise, money and room and board was given to me, naturally, because where do I have the money from?
I stayed in contact, some of them, with those people.
I knew how to-- to speak and I knew how to write a childish Swedish.
But I knew I could make myself understandable.
And there was another family way up in the north who got in contact with me and sent me parcels and wanted me to come visit them.
But I had-- How was it they were contacting you?
Was it--
That's something I forgot, too.
I don't know.
Maybe we wrote or maybe they visited.
And they wanted me to visit them, but I had no money, or I couldn't go, I couldn't take the time, whatever.
I was-- I was not able to visit them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Life in Sweden at that time was quite nice, but it was different, because everything with the traffic was on the-- yes, on the left or right-hand side, whatever.
It was opposite here.
And it was quite difficult.
But it was-- it was-- it was a good time.
I'm glad that we had time-- despite my illness, too, that I had time to a little bit be a mensch again.
Well, you were treated differently.
You got food.
You saw-- you talked with people and they treated you like you were being have to be treated and not being the fear of being killed or being shoved from here to there.
Right.
And also, you had enough food.
You could buy anything you wanted, or have everything you wanted in quantity, the quality, whatever.
That is a-- that is a lot.
So in January 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Dutch government by way of the consulate-- they knew, naturally, my whereabouts-- told me to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- I had to go on a plane.
That was the first time I made a plane trip, to Holland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That was quite nice.
The plane trip was OK the first time.
I arrived in Amsterdam and it was not so nice.
There was nobody waiting for me.
It was in the evening.
And at Schiphol at the time naturally was a mess, now still in '46-- not maturely built.
And-- OK?
